prompt:
I'm building the help-text renderer for our CLI framework and the output currently looks ragged and awful on narrow terminals. I need a proper wrap/justify engine, justwrap.py, that we own — and no, textwrap doesn't cut it (no full justification, no hyphen handling), so don't import it, write the real thing.

Two functions, both taking (text, width) and returning a list of lines:

wrap(text, width) — greedy word wrap. All whitespace in the input (spaces, tabs, newlines, runs of them) is just word separation; words on a line are joined by single spaces; a word only joins the current line if it fits within width INCLUDING its separator space. Words are never broken mid-letters — a hyphenless word longer than the width sits alone on its own overflowing line. BUT hyphenated words are fair game: when a word doesn't fit in the remaining space of the current line, first try to break it after a hyphen — take the LONGEST hyphen-terminated prefix that fits the remaining room (the hyphen stays at the end of the line, and something must remain to carry to the next line). Only if no such prefix fits does the whole word move down. Same rule on a fresh line for words longer than the width: break greedily at hyphens, longest fitting prefix each time, and the leftover keeps wrapping normally.

justify(text, width) — same wrapping, then every line EXCEPT the last is padded with spaces to exactly width, extra spaces distributed among the gaps leftmost-gap-first (so gap widths differ by at most one and the wider gaps are on the left). Lines holding a single word are left alone, and the last line is always left as-is. Hyphen-broken lines pad like any other.

Both raise ValueError for width < 1. Empty or all-whitespace text gives []. Deterministic, obviously.

test_justwrap.py is the acceptance suite — python3 test_justwrap.py, green, without touching it. Stdlib only (minus textwrap, per above).
